Kirsten Benjamin

Biochemistry and single-cell measurements

benjamin@molsci.org
510-981-8753 (w)

Roles
Research The Alpha Project
Research Research@MSI

Biography
Education
  • B.S. (Honors), Philosophy and Cellular & Molecular Biology, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, 1991
  • Ph.D., Molecular & Cell Biology, University of California at Berkeley, 1996; Ph.D. thesis title: "Biochemical Mechanisms of DNA Recombination"

    • Postdoctoral Studies

  • 1997-2003 Department of Biochemistry & Biophysics, University of California at San Francisco

    • Selected Awards & Fellowships

  • 1997-2000 Postdoctoral Fellowship, Cancer Research Fund of the Damon Runyon-Walter Winchell Foundation
  • 2000-2002 Postdoctoral Fellowship, American Cancer Society, California Division
  • 1991-1994 Graduate Fellowship, National Science Foundation
  • Elected 1991, Phi Beta Kappa, University of Michigan

    • Current Research
    To limit the universe of possible computational representations of the Alpha pheromone response pathway, it is essential to know the cellular abundance of the main components of the pathway. For example, the abundance of a molecular scaffold like Ste5 relative to its binding partners, e.g., Ste11, will determine how signals are amplified and channeled. Simulations predict that changing the kinetics of the Ste5-Ste11 binding reaction can have one effect on pathway output if Ste5 is in excess and the opposite effect if Ste11 is in excess. I am currently measuring the numbers of molecules per cell, the kinetics and affinity of protein-protein interactions, and the substrate specificity of enzymes that form the gearwork of the pathway. In addition to my protein biochemistry work, I am devising new single-cell measurements of the flow of information through the pathway.

    Publications

  • Benjamin, K.R., Zhang, C., Shokat, K.M., and Herskowitz, I. 2003. Control of landmark events in meiosis by the CDK Cdc28 and the meiosis-specific kinase Ime2. Genes Dev 17: 1524-39.
  • Clyne, R.K., Katis, V.L., Jessop, L., Benjamin, K.R., Herskowitz, I., Lichten, M., and Nasmyth, K. 2003. Polo-like kinase Cdc5 promotes chiasmata formation and cosegregation of sister centromeres at meiosis I. Nat Cell Biol 5: 480-5.
  • Pierce, M., Benjamin, K.R., Montano, S.P., Georgiadis, M.M., Winter, E., and Vershon, A.K. 2003. Sum1 and Ndt80 proteins compete for binding to middle sporulation element sequences that control meiotic gene expression. Mol Cell Biol 23: 4814-25.
  • Holmes, V.F., Scandellari, F., Benjamin, K.R., and Cozzarelli, N.R. 2002. Structure of reaction intermediates formed during Saccharomyces cerevisiae Rad51-catalyzed strand transfer. J Biol Chem 277: 38945-53.
  • Holmes, V.F., Benjamin, K.R., Crisona, N.J., and Cozzarelli, N.R. 2001. Bypass of heterology during strand transfer by Saccharomyces cerevisiae Rad51 protein. Nucleic Acids Res 29: 5052-7.
  • Benjamin, K.R., Abola, A.P., Kanaar, R., and Cozzarelli, N.R. 1996. Contributions of supercoiling to Tn3 resolvase and phage Mu Gin site-specific recombination. J Mol Biol 256: 50-65.
  • Aylsworth, C.F., Trosko, J.E., Chang, C.C., Benjamin, K., and Lockwood, E. 1989. Synergistic inhibition of metabolic cooperation by oleic acid or 12-0-tetradecanoylphorbol-13-acetate and dichlorodiphenyltrichlorethane (DDT) in Chinese hamster V79 cells: implication of a role for protein kinase C in the regulation of gap junctional intercellular communication. Cell Biol Toxicol 5: 27-37.
